title: ERP Systems
summary: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems: what they are, why they emerged, first vs second generation ERP, benefits, costs, and cloud ERP.
content_html:
ERP Systems

Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems are large-scale, integrated software applications that connect and manage processes and information flows across complex organisations into a single IT system.

Why ERPs Emerged

Historically, information was isolated within specific departments -- on individual databases, filing cabinets, or employee PCs. This caused data duplication, inaccuracies, disjointed processes, errors, lack of accountability and increased costs. Business leaders needed significant amounts of information accessible in real-time to make decisions.

Core ERP Functionality

  • Accounting and Financials (Accounts Payable/Receivable, General Ledger, Fixed Assets)
  • Human Resources (payroll, performance management, training, recruitment)
  • Production and Materials Management
  • Reporting -- single source of truth across all business functions

1st Generation ERP (1990-2010): On-Premise

Organisation buys a copy of ERP software and hosts/maintains it themselves. Supports key business functions. Expensive to acquire, implement, and maintain. Required large internal teams to administer. Vendors: Oracle, SAP, Infor, Microsoft, Sage.

2nd Generation ERP (2010+): Cloud ERP

ERP solutions hosted in cloud environments. Software as a Service (SaaS) subscription/leasing model. Vendor manages patches, upgrades, administration, and security. Lower upfront cost. Companies often run hybrid ERP solutions (on-premise + cloud).

Benefits of ERP

  • Streamlines and automates business processes.
  • Replaces manual, error-prone processes.
  • Single source of truth for data.
  • Division of responsibility managed within the application.
  • Easier reporting and decision-making.

Costs and Limitations

  • Extremely costly to buy and maintain (on-premise).
  • Complex acquisition process.
  • Difficult to integrate with existing legacy systems.
  • Significant change management required -- technology alone does not transform a business.

exercises.title: ERP definition
exercises.prompt: An ERP system is best described as:
options: A large-scale integrated software application connecting processes across an organisation
exercises.answer: A large-scale integrated software application connecting processes across an organisation

exercises.title: ERP emergence reason
exercises.prompt: ERP systems emerged primarily to solve the problem of:
options: Data isolated in separate departmental systems causing duplication and inaccuracies
Too many employees in IT departments
exercises.answer: Data isolated in separate departmental systems causing duplication and inaccuracies

exercises.title: 2nd gen ERP model
exercises.prompt: 2nd Generation (Cloud) ERP uses which commercial model?

exercises.title: 1st gen ERP vendors
exercises.prompt: Which is NOT a major traditional ERP vendor?

exercises.title: ERP single source of truth
exercises.prompt: The term 'single source of truth' in ERP context means:
options: Only one person can enter data
All business data is mastered within one integrated system ensuring consistency
The ERP vendor owns all company data
Reports can only be generated by IT staff
exercises.answer: All business data is mastered within one integrated system ensuring consistency

exercises.title: ERP limitation
exercises.prompt: A key limitation of 1st generation on-premise ERP systems was:
options: They could not handle financial data
They required internet connectivity to function
Extremely high cost to acquire, implement and maintain
exercises.answer: Extremely high cost to acquire, implement and maintain

exercises.title: Cloud ERP advantage
exercises.prompt: Compared to on-premise ERP, a key advantage of cloud ERP is:
options: The vendor manages patches, upgrades, and security -- reducing internal IT overhead
exercises.answer: The vendor manages patches, upgrades, and security -- reducing internal IT overhead

exercises.title: Hybrid ERP
exercises.prompt: A hybrid ERP environment refers to:
options: Using ERP for both HR and Finance
Running a combination of on-premise and cloud ERP solutions
exercises.answer: Running a combination of on-premise and cloud ERP solutions
